revision.text:
'''駿河国風土記'''（'''するがのくにのふどき'''）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の『[[風土記]]』である。完本は伝存せず、他書の引用によって逸文がわずかながらに伝わっており、それらの逸文を指して『駿河国風土記逸文』とも呼ばれる。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[富士川]]の流れが非常に速いので'''駿河'''という『枕詞燭明抄』上。。 ;[[三保の松原]] :昔、[[天女|神女]]が天から降りてきたが、松にかけた[[羽衣]]を漁師に奪われ、頼んでも返してくれないので、仕方なく漁師の妻となった。その後、神女は羽衣を見つけて天に帰ったが、漁師も[[仙人]]となって天に昇ったといわれる[[林羅山]]『[[本朝神社考]]』五。。 ;てこの呼坂 :「てこ」とは東国の言葉で「女」の意。昔、不来見（こぬみ）の浜に住む妻のもとに通ってくる神がいた。夫の神は岩木山を越えてやって来るが、山には荒ぶる神がいて道を通さなかったので、夫の神は荒ぶる神がいないときしか妻の神のもとに通えなかった。一方の妻の神は毎晩、山のそばまでやって来て夫神を待つのだが、なかなかやって来ないので夫神の名を呼ぶ。そこでその地を「てこの呼坂」と呼ぶのだという『続歌林良材集』上。。 ;富士の雪 :[[富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、子の刻以降に新しい雪が降るという『万葉集註釈』三。。 == 脚注 ==
== 参考文献 == *『風土記』吉野裕訳、[[平凡社]][[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]145（1969年）

'''駿河国風土記'''（'''するがのくにのふどき'''）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の『[[風土記]]』である。完本は伝存せず、他書の引用によって逸文がわずかながらに伝わっており、それらの逸文を指して『駿河国風土記逸文』とも呼ばれる。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[富士川]]の流れが非常に速いので'''駿河'''という『枕詞燭明抄』上。。 ;[[三保の松原]] :昔、[[天女|神女]]が天から降りてきたが、松にかけた[[羽衣]]を漁師に奪われ、頼んでも返してくれないので、仕方なく漁師の妻となった。その後、神女は羽衣を見つけて天に帰ったが、漁師も[[仙人]]となって天に昇ったといわれる[[林羅山]]『[[本朝神社考]]』五。。 ;てこの呼坂 :「てこ」とは東国の言葉で「女」の意。昔、不来見（こぬみ）の浜に住む妻のもとに通ってくる神がいた。夫の神は岩木山を越えてやって来るが、山には荒ぶる神がいて道を通さなかったので、夫の神は荒ぶる神がいないときしか妻の神のもとに通えなかった。一方の妻の神は毎晩、山のそばまでやって来て夫神を待つのだが、なかなかやって来ないので夫神の名を呼ぶ。そこでその地を「てこの呼坂」と呼ぶのだという『続歌林良材集』上。。 ;富士の雪 :[[富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、[[子の刻]]以降に新しい雪が降るという『万葉集註釈』三。。 == 脚注 ==
== 参考文献 == *『風土記』吉野裕訳、[[平凡社]][[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]145（1969年） {{DEFAULTSORT:するがのくにふどき}} [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]] [[Category:駿河国]] [[Category:静岡県の歴史]]

'''駿河国風土記'''（'''するがのくにのふどき'''）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の[[風土記]]である。完本は伝存せず、他書の引用によって[[逸文]]とされるものこれらの逸文とされるものは原文をそのまま引用せず、要約して掲載したものと推測される（荊木(1998)43頁および92〜93頁）。がわずかながらに伝わっており、それらを指して『駿河国風土記逸文』とも呼ばれる。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[富士川]]の流れが非常に速いので'''駿河'''という『枕詞燭明抄』上。。 ;[[三保の松原]] :昔、[[天女|神女]]が天から降りてきたが、松にかけた[[羽衣]]を漁師に奪われ、頼んでも返してくれないので、仕方なく漁師の妻となった。その後、神女は羽衣を見つけて天に帰ったが、漁師も[[仙人]]となって天に昇ったといわれる[[林羅山]]『[[本朝神社考]]』五。。 ;てこの呼坂 :「てこ」とは東国の言葉で「女」の意。昔、不来見（こぬみ）の浜に住む妻のもとに通ってくる神がいた。夫の神は岩木山を越えてやって来るが、山には荒ぶる神がいて道を通さなかったので、夫の神は荒ぶる神がいないときしか妻の神のもとに通えなかった。一方の妻の神は毎晩、山のそばまでやって来て夫神を待つのだが、なかなかやって来ないので夫神の名を呼ぶ。そこでその地を「てこの呼坂」と呼ぶのだという『続歌林良材集』上。。 ;富士の雪 :[[富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、[[子の刻]]以降に新しい雪が降るという『万葉集註釈』三。。 == 脚注 ==
== 参考文献 == *『風土記』吉野裕訳、[[平凡社]][[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]145（1969年） * 荊木美行『風土記逸文研究入門』[[国書刊行会]]（1998年） ISBN 4-336-03931-3 {{DEFAULTSORT:するかのくにふとき}} [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]] [[Category:駿河国]] [[Category:静岡県の歴史]]

revision.comment: 出典を本文中に組み込む。参考文献を著者名アイウエオ順に。その他
revision.text:
『'''駿河国風土記'''』（するがのくにのふどき）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の[[風土記]]である。現在に伝わらない[[逸書]]であるが、その[[逸文]]とされる条文がわずかながら他書に引用されこれらの逸文とされるものは原文をそのまま引用せず、要約して掲載したものと推測される（荊木(1998)43頁および92〜93頁）、それらを指して『駿河国風土記逸文』と呼ぶ。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[下河辺長流]]の『枕詞燭明抄』に「風土記に云」として引用されており、[[伴信友]]が採択古風土記とは認めがたいという（秋本）。。
[[富士川]]の流れが非常に速いので'''駿河'''と名付けられたという。 ;[[三保の松原]] :[[林羅山]]が『[[本朝神社考]]』五で「風土記を案ずるに」として引用、[[今井似閑]]が採択。
昔、[[天女|神女]]が天から降りてきたが、松にかけた[[羽衣]]を漁師に奪われ、頼んでも返してくれないので、仕方なく漁師の妻となった。その後、神女は羽衣を見つけて天に帰ったが、漁師も[[仙人]]となって天に昇ったという。[[能]]『[[羽衣 (能)|羽衣]]』でも有名な[[羽衣伝説]]の1つ。 ;てこの呼坂 :下河辺長流の『続歌林良材集』上に「するがの国の風土記に云」として引用、伴信友採択。
「てこ」とは東国の言葉で「女」の意。昔、不来見（こぬみ）の浜（[[興津川]]の河口付近の海岸）に住む妻のもとに通ってくる神がいた。夫の神は岩木山（[[薩埵山]]の古名）を越えてやって来るが、山には荒ぶる神がいて道を通さなかったので、夫の神は荒ぶる神がいないときしか妻の神のもとに通えなかった。一方の妻の神は毎晩、山のそばまでやって来て夫神を待つのだが、なかなかやって来ないので夫神の名を呼ぶ。そこでその地を「てこの呼坂」と呼ぶのだという。 ;富士の雪 :[[仙覚]]が『[[万葉集註釈]]』三において、「駿河国風土記ニミエタリト云ヘリ」と、先行書または先人の言にあったと引用、今井似閑採択。
[[富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、[[子の刻]]以降に新しい雪が降るという。 == 脚注 ==
== 参考文献 == * 秋本吉郎校注『風土記』（日本古典文学大系）、[[岩波書店]]、1958年 * 荊木美行『風土記逸文研究入門』、[[国書刊行会]]、1998年ISBN 4-336-03931-3 * 吉野裕訳『風土記』、[[平凡社]][[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]145、1969年 {{DEFAULTSORT:するかのくにふとき}} [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]] [[Category:駿河国]] [[Category:静岡県の歴史]]

『'''駿河国風土記'''』（するがのくにのふどき）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の[[風土記]]である。現在に伝わらない[[逸書]]であるが、その[[逸文]]とされる条文がわずかながら他書に引用されこれらの逸文とされるものは原文をそのまま引用せず、要約して掲載したものと推測される（荊木(1998)43頁および92〜93頁）、それらを指して『駿河国風土記逸文』と呼ぶ。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[下河辺長流]]の『枕詞燭明抄』に「風土記に云」として引用されており、[[伴信友]]が採択古風土記とは認めがたいという（秋本）。。

[[富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、[[子の刻]]以降に新しい雪が降るという。 == 脚注 ==

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Suruga Province Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga Province Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.<ref>The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries ra..."
revision.text:
'''Suruga Province Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga Province Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga Province Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.

Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]]

''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]]

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]]

''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]]

revision.text:
'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suruga no Kuni Fudoki-qq]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}[[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]]

The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.

The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. revision.text:
'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:qq]] The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.

revision.text:
'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]][[Category:qq]] '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.

Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.

The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国|12=draft}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|12=draft}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=draft}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原|12=draft}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山|12=draft}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=draft}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣|12=draft}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)|12=draft}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=draft}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=draft}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=draft}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚|12=draft}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=draft}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=draft}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店|12=draft}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=draft}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=draft}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[en:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}
