The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚|qq=draft|qq=draft}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[en:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}

revision.comment: 7 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:駿河国風土記 up to rev 79584601
revision.text:
『'''駿河国風土記'''』（するがのくにのふどき）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の[[風土記]]である。現在に伝わらない[[逸書]]であるが、その[[逸文]]とされる条文がわずかながら他書に引用されこれらの逸文とされるものは原文をそのまま引用せず、要約して掲載したものと推測される（荊木(1998)43頁および92〜93頁）、それらを指して『駿河国風土記逸文』と呼ぶ。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[下河辺長流]]の『枕詞燭明抄』に「風土記に云」として引用されており、[[伴信友]]が採択古風土記とは認めがたいという（秋本）。。
[[富士川]]の流れが非常に速いので'''駿河'''と名付けられたという。 ;[[三保の松原]] :[[林羅山]]が『[[本朝神社考]]』五で「風土記を案ずるに」として引用、[[今井似閑]]が採択。
昔、[[天女|神女]]が天から降りてきたが、松にかけた[[羽衣]]を漁師に奪われ、頼んでも返してくれないので、仕方なく漁師の妻となった。その後、神女は羽衣を見つけて天に帰ったが、漁師も[[仙人]]となって天に昇ったという。[[能]]『[[羽衣 (能)|羽衣]]』でも有名な[[羽衣伝説]]の1つ。 ;てこの呼坂 :下河辺長流の『続歌林良材集』上に「するがの国の風土記に云」として引用、伴信友採択。
「てこ」とは東国の言葉で「女」の意。昔、不来見（こぬみ）の浜（[[興津川]]の河口付近の海岸）に住む妻のもとに通ってくる神がいた。夫の神は岩木山（[[薩埵峠|薩埵山]]の古名）を越えてやって来るが、山には荒ぶる神がいて道を通さなかったので、夫の神は荒ぶる神がいないときしか妻の神のもとに通えなかった。一方の妻の神は毎晩、山のそばまでやって来て夫神を待つのだが、なかなかやって来ないので夫神の名を呼ぶ。そこでその地を「てこの呼坂」と呼ぶのだという。 ;富士の雪 :[[仙覚]]が『[[万葉集註釈]]』三において、「駿河国風土記ニミエタリト云ヘリ」と、先行書または先人の言にあったと引用、今井似閑採択。
[[富士山 (代表的なトピック)|富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、[[子の刻]]以降に新しい雪が降るという。 == 脚注 ==
== 参考文献 == * 秋本吉郎校注『風土記』（日本古典文学大系）、[[岩波書店]]、1958年 * 荊木美行『風土記逸文研究入門』、[[国書刊行会]]、1998年 ISBN 4-336-03931-3 * 吉野裕訳『風土記』、[[平凡社]][[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]145、1969年 {{DEFAULTSORT:するかのくにふとき}} [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]] [[Category:駿河国]] [[Category:静岡県の歴史]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[駿河国風土記]] to [[Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
『'''駿河国風土記'''』（するがのくにのふどき）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[駿河国]]の[[風土記]]である。現在に伝わらない[[逸書]]であるが、その[[逸文]]とされる条文がわずかながら他書に引用されこれらの逸文とされるものは原文をそのまま引用せず、要約して掲載したものと推測される（荊木(1998)43頁および92〜93頁）、それらを指して『駿河国風土記逸文』と呼ぶ。 == 逸文 == ;駿河の国号の由来 :[[下河辺長流]]の『枕詞燭明抄』に「風土記に云」として引用されており、[[伴信友]]が採択古風土記とは認めがたいという（秋本）。。
[[富士川]]の流れが非常に速いので'''駿河'''と名付けられたという。 ;[[三保の松原]] :[[林羅山]]が『[[本朝神社考]]』五で「風土記を案ずるに」として引用、[[今井似閑]]が採択。
昔、[[天女|神女]]が天から降りてきたが、松にかけた[[羽衣]]を漁師に奪われ、頼んでも返してくれないので、仕方なく漁師の妻となった。その後、神女は羽衣を見つけて天に帰ったが、漁師も[[仙人]]となって天に昇ったという。[[能]]『[[羽衣 (能)|羽衣]]』でも有名な[[羽衣伝説]]の1つ。 ;てこの呼坂 :下河辺長流の『続歌林良材集』上に「するがの国の風土記に云」として引用、伴信友採択。
「てこ」とは東国の言葉で「女」の意。昔、不来見（こぬみ）の浜（[[興津川]]の河口付近の海岸）に住む妻のもとに通ってくる神がいた。夫の神は岩木山（[[薩埵峠|薩埵山]]の古名）を越えてやって来るが、山には荒ぶる神がいて道を通さなかったので、夫の神は荒ぶる神がいないときしか妻の神のもとに通えなかった。一方の妻の神は毎晩、山のそばまでやって来て夫神を待つのだが、なかなかやって来ないので夫神の名を呼ぶ。そこでその地を「てこの呼坂」と呼ぶのだという。 ;富士の雪 :[[仙覚]]が『[[万葉集註釈]]』三において、「駿河国風土記ニミエタリト云ヘリ」と、先行書または先人の言にあったと引用、今井似閑採択。
[[富士山 (代表的なトピック)|富士山]]の雪は6月15日になくなり、[[子の刻]]以降に新しい雪が降るという。 == 脚注 ==
== 参考文献 == * 秋本吉郎校注『風土記』（日本古典文学大系）、[[岩波書店]]、1958年 * 荊木美行『風土記逸文研究入門』、[[国書刊行会]]、1998年 ISBN 4-336-03931-3 * 吉野裕訳『風土記』、[[平凡社]][[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]145、1969年 {{DEFAULTSORT:するかのくにふとき}} [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]] [[Category:駿河国]] [[Category:静岡県の歴史]]

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚|qq=draft|qq=draft}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[en:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚|qq=draft|qq=draft}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|ja|三保の松原|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|hagoromo (feathered robe)|ja|羽衣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Senkaku|ja|仙覚|qq=draft|qq=draft}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[en:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]]

The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|qq=}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|qq=}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|qq=}}, 1998. '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|qq=}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|qq=}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|qq=}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|qq=}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|qq=}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|qq=}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|qq=}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|qq=}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[en:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:風土記]]

ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|qq=}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]]

'''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|qq=}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|qq=}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|qq=}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|qq=}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|qq=}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Suruga no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]]

Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|qq=|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|qq=|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11668491]] Wikidata name: Suruga no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文|1=lost literary work|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1585442}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|qq=|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|qq=|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|qq=|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|qq=|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|qq=|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|qq=|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}}

The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}}

[[ja:駿河国風土記]][[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668491]] Wikidata name: Suruga no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文|1=lost literary work|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1585442}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}}

''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:駿河国風土記]][[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668491]] Wikidata name: Suruga no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文|1=lost literary work|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1585442}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ja:駿河国風土記]][[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668491]] Wikidata name: Suruga no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文|1=lost literary work|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1585442}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]]

[[ja:駿河国風土記]][[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668491]] Wikidata name: Suruga no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.

Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. [[ja:駿河国風土記]][[zh:駿河國風土記]] [[ja:駿河国風土記]][[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668491]] Wikidata name: Suruga no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki''' (駿河国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文|1=lost literary work|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1585442}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]]. ; [[:en:Miho no Matsubara|Miho no Matsubara]] Quoted by [[:en:Hayashi Razan|Hayashi Razan]] in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her [[:en:Hagoromo|hagoromo (feathered robe)]] on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''[[:en:Hagoromo (play)|Hagoromo (Noh)]].'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji [[:en:Sengaku|Senkaku]] quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|en|Fuji River|WD=Q596110|lt=Fuji River}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|en|Miho no Matsubara|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|WD=Q558279|lt=Hayashi Razan}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|Hagoromo|en|Hagoromo|WD=Q1208551|lt=hagoromo (feathered robe)}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (play)|en|Hagoromo (play)|WD=Q3125720|lt=Hagoromo (Noh)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Sengaku|en|Sengaku|WD=Q7450531|lt=Senkaku}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==
== Further Reading == * Akimoto, Yoshio, ed. ''Fudoki'' (Japanese Classical Literature Series). {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1958. * Eganoki, Miyuki. ''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is considered a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lost work|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 逸文|1=Lost literary work|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1585442}}, though some fragments of its text have been quoted in other sources. These excerpts, referred to as the ''Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' (駿河国風土記逸文), provide insights into the content of the original text.The quoted fragments are believed to be summaries rather than verbatim reproductions of the original text (Eganoki, 1998, pp. 43, 92–93). == Fragments == ; Origin of the Name "Suruga" Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Makurakotoba Shokumeishō'' as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.Akimoto suggests that this might not be from an authentic Fudoki.
The name "Suruga" (駿河) was said to derive from the fast-flowing currents of the {{ill|Fuji River|en|Fuji River|WD=Q596110|lt=Fuji River|1=Fuji River}}. ; {{ill|Miho no Matsubara|en|Miho no Matsubara|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}} Quoted by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|WD=Q558279|lt=Hayashi Razan|1=Hayashi Razan}} in his ''Honchō Jinjashō'' Volume 5 as "according to the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
A celestial maiden (神女) descended from heaven and hung her {{ill|Hagoromo|en|Hagoromo|WD=Q1208551|lt=hagoromo (feathered robe)|1=Hagoromo}} on a pine tree, but a fisherman took it and refused to return it despite her pleas. Resigned, the maiden became the fisherman's wife. Eventually, she found her robe and returned to heaven, with the fisherman later ascending to the heavens as an immortal. This tale is one of the many versions of the famous ''Hagoromo Legend,'' also celebrated in the Noh play ''{{ill|Hagoromo (play)|en|Hagoromo (play)|WD=Q3125720|lt=Hagoromo (Noh)|1=Hagoromo (play)}}.'' ; Tekono Yobisaka (The Calling Slope of Tekono) Quoted in Shimokawabe Nagaru's ''Zoku Karin Ryōzaishū'' Volume 1 as "mentioned in the Fudoki" and adopted by {{ill|Tomo no Nobutomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomo no Nobutomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}}.
"Tekono" was an Eastern word for "woman." Long ago, a deity who lived on the shore of Konumi Beach (near the mouth of the {{ill|Okitsu River|ja|興津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsu River|1=Okitsu River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613417}}) waited for her husband to visit. The husband deity had to cross Iwaki Mountain (the old name for {{ill|Satta Pass|ja|薩埵峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satta Pass|1=Satta Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622261}}), where a violent deity roamed. He could only visit when the violent deity was absent. The wife deity waited nightly near the mountain, calling out for her husband, who often did not come. The place became known as "Tekono Yobisaka" (The Calling Slope of Tekono). ; Snow of Mount Fuji {{ill|Sengaku|en|Sengaku|WD=Q7450531|lt=Senkaku|1=Sengaku}} quoted in ''Man'yōshū Chūshaku'' Volume 3, stating "it appears in the Suruga no Kuni Fudoki" as mentioned by earlier sources and adopted by {{ill|Imai Nikan|ja|今井似閑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nikan|1=Imai Jikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535865}}.
The snow on {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山 (代表的なトピック)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士山|1=Mount Fuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q39231}} was said to melt completely by the 15th day of the 6th month, only to see fresh snowfall after the hour of the rat (midnight). == References ==

''Introduction to Research on Fudoki Fragments.'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankōkai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankōkai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}, 1998. ISBN 4-336-03931-3. * Yoshino, Yutaka, trans. ''Fudoki.'' {{ill|Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja|平凡社東洋文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha Toyo Bunko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東洋文庫 (平凡社)|1=Q11527658|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527658}} 145, 1969. == Related Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/ Suruga no Kuni Fudoki Fragments]'' in digital format, National Diet Library. {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|駿河国風土記|version=79584601|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:駿河国風土記]] [[zh:駿河國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11668491}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Suruga Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]
